description: Lock advanced_shipbuilding_bireme_trireme to the sixth-century-BCE Greek trireme and its oar, timber, cordage, passage-sail, and bronze-ram architecture.

checks.reason: The construction node represents monumental stone and civic works rather than a ship-specific fabrication capability.
No reviewed trireme evidence makes iron indispensable to the hull, propulsion, rig, or bronze ram.
The defining trireme propulsion system used 170 manually worked oars arranged on three levels.
Without manual oar propulsion the scoped vessel is no longer a trireme.
The lightweight vessel depended critically on a shaped and joined timber hull.
Timber construction is constitutive of the historically evidenced trireme hull.
Hypozomata braced the hull and were standard commissioned equipment in Athenian naval inventories.
A commissioned operational trireme required its heavy hull-bracing cordage.
The vessel carried square-rigged sails for passages and could operate under wind power.
Sails extended passage capability but did not replace the defining three-level oar system.
Archaeological evidence directly supports a cast-bronze prow ram on the three-tier warship tradition.
Bronze enabled the offensive ram without constituting the timber hull or oar arrangement.
The reviewed reconstruction summaries attest triremes from at least the sixth century BCE while exact invention remains disputed.
